Innovative Statewide ATPs

What Sets Our Plans Apart

Five selected plans from Toole Design’s statewide ATP portfolio illustrate some of the range and depth of innovation our team brings — from network design frameworks and funding scenario tools to interactive online plan delivery and ongoing implementation programs. Click any image to enlarge.

State 01  ·  New Mexico DOT

New Mexico Active Transportation Plan

NMDOT ATP  In Development
Network Design

Revised Corridor Tier Framework — More Nuanced, More Connected

Corridor Tier classification framework

New Mexico’s bicycle planning establishes a Corridor Tier system for classifying U.S. and New Mexico state highways. The prior New Mexico Bicycle Plan assigned tiers based on potential level of use and public benefit — a solid foundation, but one that left a gap between tier designation and design practice. Toole Design is significantly expanding this system in the new ATP, reassigning every US and NM state highway using a more nuanced methodology that integrates land use context zones, trip potential, and proximity to key destinations.

The revised framework links corridor tiers directly to specific design users — reflecting what level of interaction between pedestrians, bicyclists, and vehicle traffic is appropriate along each corridor. This creates a clear, deliberate chain: land use context → corridor tier → design user → expected facility type.

Design Guidance

Design Expectations — One Home for All Multimodal Design Guidance

Design Expectations framework

Toole Design is creating a dedicated Design Expectations document for NMDOT that becomes the authoritative source for bicycle and pedestrian facility selection and design parameters statewide. Critically, the NMDOT Design Manual is being updated to point to this document for multimodal design guidance rather than duplicating or scattering it across multiple manuals — a structural change with lasting impact on how NMDOT staff and consultants access and apply standards.

Design Expectations are driven by the intersection of three inputs — User Types, Corridor Tier, and Land Use. The document includes facility selection guidance, design parameters (widths, buffers, shy distance), intersection and crossing treatments, and decision-making tools, consistent with AASHTO standards but tailored to New Mexico.

Practical Application

Design Applications — Guidance Through Real New Mexico Examples

Design Applications examples
Interconnected guidance ecosystem
ATP Components overview

Toole Design is developing Design Applications — case study examples that apply design guidance to recurring challenging conditions specific to New Mexico: highways functioning as community Main Streets, rural corridors through tribal lands (NM 4, Jemez Pueblo), and urban locations with constrained right-of-way (US 54, Carrizozo). This bridges the gap between policy and practice for NMDOT district engineers and designers.

Systems Integration

An Interconnected Guidance Ecosystem

Toole Design designed the New Mexico ATP as part of a deliberate, integrated system of three NMDOT documents that work together:

DocumentRoleAnswers
Context Sensitive Solutions (CSS) ChapterDefines considerations for context-sensitive design; identifies land use context zonesWhy & Where
ATP + Quick Build GuideFacility types, design expectations, and quick-build treatments for bicycle & pedestrian designWhat & How
Design ManualNMDOT’s overarching engineering standards — updated to refer to ATP for multimodal design decisionsMechanics
State 02  ·  Kansas DOT

Kansas Active Transportation Plan

KDOT ATP  ·  February 2023  ·  View Plan →
Local Capacity Building

Active Transportation Planning Toolkit for Small and Mid-Sized Communities

ATP Planning Toolkit cover

Recognizing that most Kansas communities lack in-house planning capacity, Toole Design produced a companion planning toolkit specifically for communities with populations up to 20,000. Rather than expecting small communities to hire consultants, Toole Design developed a practical, self-guided resource walking community staff through every phase of active transportation planning.

The toolkit includes step-by-step planning guidance, worksheets, stakeholder engagement tools, sample meeting agendas, an Active Transportation Infrastructure Visual Glossary, and an Active Transportation Plan Template in PowerPoint format. A standout companion piece: the Guide to Creating Active Transportation Maps Using Microsoft PowerPoint enables communities to produce professional-quality network maps using free tools — no GIS expertise or software licenses required.

Engagement strategies matrix from the toolkit
Tourism & Economic Development

Active Tourism Toolkit

Active Tourism Toolkit cover

Toole Design developed a standalone Active Tourism Toolkit to help Kansas communities leverage their walking, bicycling, and trail assets for economic development and tourism. Most statewide ATPs treat tourism as a footnote; Toole Design produced a dedicated, actionable toolkit covering:

  • What active tourism is and why it matters economically
  • How to identify target markets and capitalize on existing assets
  • Methods for promotion, marketing, and wayfinding
  • How to measure success and track economic impact
  • Noteworthy examples and case studies from across the country
Ongoing Implementation

Walk Bike Roll Kansas — Continuing Education and Capacity Building

Rather than treating the ATP as a “publish and shelve” document, KDOT created — with Toole Design’s support — a continuing education infrastructure to actively implement it year-round:

  • Monthly virtual webinars covering specific implementation topics — Active Tourism, Demonstration Projects, Toolkit Use, Rural Communities, Safe Routes to School, Speed Management, and more — all recorded and archived for ongoing access
  • Annual KATE Active Transportation Summit: a professional in-person conference offering up to 6.25 AICP continuing education credits, including optional field activities and a dedicated Transportation Alternatives Program workshop for practitioners
  • All session materials archived via SharePoint, creating a growing, searchable public knowledge library

This model positions KDOT as an active implementation partner every month of the year, organized around six strategic priorities: Safety, Equity, Mobility, Community Health, Culture Shift, and System Longevity.

Supporting Analysis

Statewide Economic Impact Analysis

The Kansas ATP also includes a statewide economic impact analysis quantifying existing and potential benefits of active transportation across four categories (tourism, retail, transportation, and facility access). This analysis — led by a specialized subconsultant — speaks to the language of legislators and agency leadership and produced a replicable Benefit-Cost Tool and User Guide that local communities can use independently to quantify returns from future projects.

State 03  ·  Oregon DOT

Oregon Bicycle and Pedestrian Plan

ODOT OBPP  ·  Adopted May 2016  ·  View Plan →
Investment Planning

Transparent Investment Funding Scenarios

For the Oregon Bicycle and Pedestrian Plan, Toole Design developed a notably candid section presenting four distinct investment funding scenarios, each describing what outcomes are realistically achievable at different annual funding levels through 2040. This approach is unusual among statewide ATPs, which typically present recommendations without tying them to funding realities or acknowledging trade-offs explicitly.

The stated intent is to help decision-makers understand priorities and to “be transparent about the negative and positive consequences of funding levels” — treating policymakers and the public as informed partners rather than passive audiences.

Oregon Bicycle and Pedestrian Plan cover
Funding scenarios overview
ScenarioAnnual Funding25-Year TotalOutlook
1 — TriageUp to $18.5M$440MFunding declines; only urgent maintenance and safety fixes; system quality deteriorates; new connections extremely limited
2 — Status QuoUp to $29M$700MCurrent trajectory; preservation continues but falls behind; marginal gap-filling; much of the network remains disconnected
3 — Phase IUp to $38M$900MSystematic improvements possible; network gaps begin closing; safety broadens; equity improves; system performance meaningfully better
4 — Phase IIUp to $105M$2.5BPlan vision largely achievable; well-connected network; system can pace population growth; more costly comfort improvements become possible
Scenario 1: Triage (reduced funding)
Scenario 3: Phase I (increased funding)

By naming and describing these scenarios in plain language — including the consequences of underfunding — this plan gives decision-makers and advocates a concrete, honest framework for investment conversations.

State 04  ·  Washington State DOT

Cycle Highways Action Plan

WSDOT  ·  Phase 1, June 2025  ·  View Plan →
Concept Innovation

Defining “Cycle Highways” — A New Category of Transportation Infrastructure

Cycle Highways Action Plan cover

The central innovation of the Washington plan is the concept itself: cycle highways — a named, multi-jurisdictional network of facilities that does for bicyclists what the state highway system does for drivers.

“Washington cycle highways are a multi-jurisdictional network of facilities that optimize bicycle, micromobility, and other forms of active travel through and between population centers to support existing active travel demand and encourage mode shift from personal motor vehicles to active transportation. Cycle highways are well-maintained facilities that include shared-use paths, paved trails, bike lanes, and bicycle boulevards that emphasize safety, reduce modal conflicts, and prioritize efficient active transportation travel.”

Like a state highway, a cycle highway is expected to be consistent in quality, safe, direct, and usable for trips of any length or frequency. Facilities within the network are held to uniform standards for traffic stress (LTS 2 or better), wayfinding, lighting, and maintenance — creating a reliable, predictable user experience across jurisdictions.

Executive Summary, Cycle Highways Action Plan Phase 1 (June 2025)
Program Structure

WSDOT as Backbone Organization and Phased Implementation

The plan recommends establishing a permanent, staffed Cycle Highways Program within WSDOT — functioning like WSDOT’s existing aviation and public transportation programs — to coordinate the network across all jurisdictions.

Phase 2 action steps and estimated costs

The action plan is structured in deliberate phases:

  • Phase 1 (this document): Framework, state of practice, stakeholder engagement, cost estimates — funded by ESHB 2134 (2023–25 biennium)
  • Phase 2: Full Action Plan, preliminary network definition, design and technical guidance, pilot projects, and program launch (~6 new FTEs + $500K/year)
  • Phase 3: Full network buildout with partner agency agreements, capital construction, long-term maintenance programming
State 05  ·  Massachusetts DOT

Massachusetts Bicycle & Pedestrian Plans

MassDOT  ·  2019  ·  Bike Plan →  Ped Plan →
Note: Toole Design produced the 2019 Massachusetts Bicycle Transportation Plan (and the companion Pedestrian Plan) for MassDOT. MassDOT has updated and expanded both plans since then.
Massachusetts Bicycle Transportation Plan
Plan contents and structure (ArcGIS StoryMap)
Bikeway selection matrix
Delivery Innovation

A Plan Without a PDF — Delivered Entirely Online

The MassDOT Bike Plan exists entirely online as an ArcGIS MapJournal Story Map — there is no traditional PDF document to download or print. The plan is read and navigated as an interactive, scrolling web experience. This delivery model is itself the innovation: rather than producing a static snapshot that ages the moment it is published, MassDOT built active transportation planning infrastructure — a suite of living, interactive data tools that remain useful and current long after adoption.

Instead of asking stakeholders to read a report, MassDOT built tools they can actually use — and that MassDOT can update and maintain over time.
Data-Driven Tools

Interactive Web Applications as the Core of the Plan

Bicycle & Pedestrian Infrastructure Gap Analysis Web Map

A live ArcGIS Experience Builder application identifying physical gaps (missing facilities) and quality gaps (substandard existing facilities) across all MassDOT-maintained roadways, organized by 13 regional planning areas. Produces a prioritized project list in mid-term and long-term investment tiers.

Potential for Everyday Biking Model

A statistical model — built on INRIX big data and open-source GIS — identifying MassDOT roadways with the highest latent demand for bikeway investment. Updated in 2022; published as open data on the MassDOT Open Data Portal with guidance for all 13 MPOs on local application.

Priority Trails Network Vision Map

An interactive statewide map tracking ~320 miles of completed rail trails, 24 miles under construction, and 60+ funded-but-unbuilt miles. A living inventory, not a frozen snapshot.

GeoDOT Web Application

A statewide bicycle facility inventory tool incorporating the Potential for Everyday Biking data. Supports connected network planning for MPOs and local agencies and is used as a reference across all 13 regional planning areas.

Local Capacity

Statewide Tools With Local Application

The plan includes a Municipal Resource Guide for Bikeability targeting all 351 Massachusetts cities and towns, with direct connections to MassDOT grant programs that fund implementation:

  • The Complete Streets Funding Program has awarded 278 Tier 3 construction grants totaling over $100 million, providing a direct pipeline from the plan’s gap analysis to funded local projects
  • Safe Routes to School Signs and Lines Grant and SRTS Infrastructure Grant programs
  • Guidance for all 13 MPOs on how to apply the Potential for Everyday Biking model locally

Designing communities where people can live, move, and thrive.
